Authors: Masseti, M
Article Title: Mammals of the Mediterranean islands: homogenisation and the loss of biodiversity
notes: Results taken from the text where it describes how humans erased distinct endemic faunas and replaced them with a broadly uniform suite of introduced, generalist mammals, especially rodents and pastoral species. Discusses the processes for all islands in the region rather than within specific archipelagos

Authors: Mitchell, SL; Edwards, DP; Martin, RW; Deere, NJ; Voigt, M; Kastanya, A; Karja, A; Akbar, PG; Jordan, K; Tasirin, J; Zakaria, Z; Martin, T; Supriatna, J; Winarni, N; Davies, ZG; Struebig, MJ
Article Title: Severity of deforestation mediates biotic homogenisation in an island archipelago
notes: Not a true inter-island comparison using full island-level species lists nor a temporal analysis, but instead compares between islands along a forest cover gradient in a sort of space-for-time substitution approach. We have extrapoloated the results to infer biotic homogenisation at the island level on basis of Figure 5 and statements in the text, e.g., "highlight how continued conversion of forests is causing the replacement of endemic species by a small cohort of ubiquitous species shared across all islands" on the basis that these ubiquitous species across all islands will have reduced inter-island beta-diversity.

Authors: Kennedy, S; Calaor, J; Zurápiti, Y; Hans, J; Yoshimura, M; Choo, J; Andersen, JC; Callaghan, J; Roderick, GK; Krehenwinkel, H; Rogers, H; Gillespie, RG; Economo, EP
Article Title: Richness and resilience in the Pacific: DNA metabarcoding enables parallelized evaluation of biogeographic patterns
notes: Main analyses are of beta-div within islands, so ignored here, but they also compare across their three islands / archipelagos (Okinawa, Marianas, Hawaii), are looking at the effect of introduced species and results in Section 4.3 indicate the introduced species are different on each island, leading to differentiation. Uses OTUs and not a phylogeny to calculate beta-div, so arguably closer to taxonomic rather than phylogenetic beta-diversity

Authors: Rosenblad, KC; Sax, DF
Article Title: A new framework for investigating biotic homogenization and exploring future trajectories: oceanic island plant and bird assemblages as a case study
notes: Pairwise analysis of Jaccard's similarity of 20 islands: 154 out 190 pairwise comparisons homogenised, so result taken as homogenisation

Authors: Rosenblad, KC; Sax, DF
Article Title: A new framework for investigating biotic homogenization and exploring future trajectories: oceanic island plant and bird assemblages as a case study

notes: analysis compared each archipelago with all others pairwise - all pairwise comparisons bar 2 show increased similarity, so we have considered homogenisation in all cases. Different scale of analysis (i.e., between archipelago) and included islands than in Castro et al (2007, 2008)

Authors: Florencio, M; Cardoso, P; Lobo, JM; de Azevedo, EB; Borges, PAV
Article Title: Arthropod assemblage homogenization in oceanic islands: the role of indigenous and exotic species under landscape disturbance
notes: Compares compositional similarity (Bray-Curtis similarity, thus includes abundance data) of just natives and then native+exotics (epigean arthropods), for four Azorean islands. Results in Table 3 show that average similarity increased with addition of exotics for all habitats, and then for all of the four land-use types (native forest, exotic forest and the pastures). Table S1 also indicates that similarity increased in all individual pairwise comparisons.

Authors: Florencio, M; Lobo, JM; Cardoso, P; Almeida-Neto, M; Borges, PAV
Article Title: The Colonisation of Exotic Species Does Not Have to Trigger Faunal Homogenisation: Lessons from the Assembly Patterns of Arthropods on Oceanic Islands
notes: Similar dataset to Florencio et al (2013) but different analytical approach (here using a different replacement index and only incidence data) and no all habitat analysis, but different comparisons within the four land-use types across the four islands. All significant pairwise comparisons in pastures (both types) and exotic forest show differentiation when exotics added to indigenous, with four native forest comparisons showing homogenisation (meaning 18/22 of significant comparisons showing differentiation). Also, the average values for two pastures and exotic forest show differentiation, and so main result taken to be differentiation.

Authors: Longman, EK; Rosenblad, K; Sax, DF
Article Title: Extreme homogenization: The past, present and future of mammal assemblages on islands
notes: Change in similarity across global oceanic islands

Authors: Longman, EK; Rosenblad, K; Sax, DF
Article Title: Extreme homogenization: The past, present and future of mammal assemblages on islands
notes: Change in similarity across global continental islands

Authors: Cassey, P; Lockwood, JL; Blackburn, TM; Olden, JD
Article Title: Spatial scale and evolutionary history determine the degree of taxonomic homogenization across island bird assemblages
notes: Result taken from this sentence "Islands in the Atlantic Ocean tend to differentiate in relation to other islands within their own archipelago, but homogenize when compared to islands outside their archipelago" - and also confirmed using results provided by Xianping Li (who used the same underlying database); pairwise analysis (using Jaccard similarity) and we used a threshold of >70% of pairwise analyses to indicate homogenisation, differentation, no change, or if not >70%, mixed

Authors: Li, XP; Bellard, C; Hu, F; Li, HX
Article Title: Effect of distance, area, and climate on the frequency of introduction and extinction events on islands and archipelagos
notes: Uses same data as Cassey et al, but this includes the global analysis (i.e., change in mean pairwise similarity across all islands)

Authors: Ijima, D; Ando, H; Inoue, T; Murakami, M; Ito, S; Fukunda, S; Sato, NJ
Article Title:
Ongoing collapse of avifauna in temperate oceanic islands close
to the mainland in the Anthropocene
notes: Pairwise analysis based on Jaccard dissimilarity - results taken from text and Figure 5, where it looks like almost all island pairwise comparisons have become more dissimilar

Authors: Triantis, K.A., Rigal, F., Whittaker, R.J., Hume, J.P., Sheard, C., Poursanidis, D., Rolland, J., Sfenthourakis, S., Matthews, T.J., Thébaud, C. & Tobias, J.A.
Article Title: Deterministic assembly and anthropogenic extinctions drive convergence of island bird communities.
notes: Pairwise analysis (using modified version of MNTD for turnover) of archipelagos, with analysis running just using native extant avifauna, and the native avifauna + extinct species. Pairwise results provided by Francois Rigal, and show that 70/153 of pairwise comparison = differentiation, and so result taken as mixed (as not pattern > 70%), which was confirmed by Francois Rigal.

Authors: Carvallo, G. O. & Castro, S. A.
Article Title: Invasions but not extinctions change phylogenetic diversity of angiosperm assemblage on southeastern Pacific Oceanic islands
notes: Same islands (and island groups) as in the other Castro et al papers, but here looking at phylogenetic beta-diversity. Pairwise analysis between islands (using 1-PhyloSOR metric) and pairwise matrices for the pre-European (including extinct) and current (excluding extinct and including exotics) floras provided by Gaston Carvalho.

Authors: Carvallo, G. O. & Castro, S. A.
Article Title: Invasions but not extinctions change phylogenetic diversity of angiosperm assemblage on southeastern Pacific Oceanic islands
notes: Same islands (and island groups) as in the other Castro et al papers, but here looking at phylogenetic beta-diversity. Pairwise analysis between islands (using 1-PhyloSOR metric) and pairwise matrices for the pre-European (including extinct) and current (excluding extinct and including exotics) floras provided by Gaston Carvalho. No pattern occured in >70% of pairwise comparison, so result took to be mixed.
